channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK mortgage and housing finance news, sourced and rewritten daily.

item.title: How much worse could Iran war make the cost of living crisis? – The Latest
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
```html

By Bambang Setiawan

How Middle East tensions could impact UK household finances

Escalating conflict in the Middle East has raised concerns about potential knock-on effects for UK households already grappling with high living costs. Financial experts warn that sustained geopolitical instability could lead to increased energy bills, higher petrol prices and continued pressure on mortgage rates.

Key areas of financial concern

Homeowners might notice several potential impacts on their budgets:

  • Energy costs: Global oil price fluctuations could feed through to domestic gas and electricity bills
  • Fuel prices: Petrol and diesel costs may rise if supply chains are disrupted
  • Mortgage rates: Lenders could adjust products in response to market volatility
  • Inflation: Persistent upward pressure might delay anticipated Bank of England rate cuts

Navigating uncertain financial times

For those concerned about these potential financial pressures, several approaches might be worth considering:

  • Reviewing energy tariffs when the price cap next changes
  • Exploring fixed-rate mortgage options if coming to the end of a current deal
  • Building emergency savings where possible to cushion against price shocks
  • Monitoring fuel price trends to identify optimal times to fill up

While the full financial impact remains uncertain, staying informed about market developments could help UK households prepare for potential challenges ahead. Financial advisers typically recommend reviewing budgets and considering appropriate protections against rising costs.

```
item.content:encoded:
```html

By Bambang Setiawan

How Middle East tensions could impact UK household finances

Escalating conflict in the Middle East has raised concerns about potential knock-on effects for UK households already grappling with high living costs. Financial experts warn that sustained geopolitical instability could lead to increased energy bills, higher petrol prices and continued pressure on mortgage rates.

Key areas of financial concern

Homeowners might notice several potential impacts on their budgets:

  • Energy costs: Global oil price fluctuations could feed through to domestic gas and electricity bills
  • Fuel prices: Petrol and diesel costs may rise if supply chains are disrupted
  • Mortgage rates: Lenders could adjust products in response to market volatility
  • Inflation: Persistent upward pressure might delay anticipated Bank of England rate cuts

Navigating uncertain financial times

For those concerned about these potential financial pressures, several approaches might be worth considering:

  • Reviewing energy tariffs when the price cap next changes
  • Exploring fixed-rate mortgage options if coming to the end of a current deal
  • Building emergency savings where possible to cushion against price shocks
  • Monitoring fuel price trends to identify optimal times to fill up

While the full financial impact remains uncertain, staying informed about market developments could help UK households prepare for potential challenges ahead. Financial advisers typically recommend reviewing budgets and considering appropriate protections against rising costs.

```

item.title: Average UK mortgage rate tops 5% as lenders scurry to reprice loans amid Middle East crisis
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Average mortgage rates in the UK have surged past 5%, marking a significant shift in the housing market amidst growing global uncertainty. The escalation of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East has triggered a swift response from lenders, leading to widespread repricing of mortgage products. Over the past 48 hours, nearly 500 deals have been withdrawn, creating one of the most turbulent periods since the aftermath of the 2022 mini-budget.

Market Turmoil Sparks Lender Action

Major high-street lenders, including HSBC, Nationwide, Halifax, and Barclays, have raised rates across their mortgage offerings. HSBC, for instance, announced a second round of price increases, set to take effect from Thursday. This rapid adjustment reflects the volatility in swap rates, which lenders use to price fixed-rate mortgages. Adam French, head of consumer finance at Moneyfacts, described the situation as "some of the most turbulent days in the UK mortgage market since late 2022."

French noted that while the scale of product withdrawals has been significant, it remains below the levels seen during the mini-budget crisis, when 935 deals were pulled in a single day. Despite this, the average two-year fixed-rate mortgage has climbed to 5.01%, up from 4.84% just before the recent conflict. Similarly, the typical five-year fixed rate now stands at 5.09%, echoing the highs of last summer.

Impact on Borrowers and the Broader Economy

The rise in mortgage costs poses challenges for both prospective buyers and those looking to remortgage. With approximately 1.8 million fixed-rate deals set to expire in 2026, many homeowners may face higher repayments when securing new loans. The shift in mortgage pricing comes against a backdrop of global economic uncertainty, driven by the Middle East conflict. Before the crisis, economists had anticipated potential rate cuts in 2026. However, concerns over rising oil and gas prices reigniting inflation have altered these expectations.

Swap rates, which influence mortgage pricing, have climbed sharply as markets grapple with this uncertainty. Financial experts now predict that the Bank of England will hold the base rate at 3.75% during its March meeting, a stark contrast to earlier forecasts of a potential reduction. The likelihood of a rate cut this year has fallen from 50% to just 20% in recent days, leaving borrowers in a less favourable position than previously hoped.

Households had been enjoying lower mortgage rates in recent months, but this trend has quickly reversed. French highlighted that the sudden shift is unwelcome news for borrowers, with further increases dependent on global market developments. As lenders continue to adapt to the evolving landscape, homeowners and buyers may wish to stay informed and explore their options carefully in this unpredictable environment.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Average mortgage rates in the UK have surged past 5%, marking a significant shift in the housing market amidst growing global uncertainty. The escalation of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East has triggered a swift response from lenders, leading to widespread repricing of mortgage products. Over the past 48 hours, nearly 500 deals have been withdrawn, creating one of the most turbulent periods since the aftermath of the 2022 mini-budget.

Market Turmoil Sparks Lender Action

Major high-street lenders, including HSBC, Nationwide, Halifax, and Barclays, have raised rates across their mortgage offerings. HSBC, for instance, announced a second round of price increases, set to take effect from Thursday. This rapid adjustment reflects the volatility in swap rates, which lenders use to price fixed-rate mortgages. Adam French, head of consumer finance at Moneyfacts, described the situation as "some of the most turbulent days in the UK mortgage market since late 2022."

French noted that while the scale of product withdrawals has been significant, it remains below the levels seen during the mini-budget crisis, when 935 deals were pulled in a single day. Despite this, the average two-year fixed-rate mortgage has climbed to 5.01%, up from 4.84% just before the recent conflict. Similarly, the typical five-year fixed rate now stands at 5.09%, echoing the highs of last summer.

Impact on Borrowers and the Broader Economy

The rise in mortgage costs poses challenges for both prospective buyers and those looking to remortgage. With approximately 1.8 million fixed-rate deals set to expire in 2026, many homeowners may face higher repayments when securing new loans. The shift in mortgage pricing comes against a backdrop of global economic uncertainty, driven by the Middle East conflict. Before the crisis, economists had anticipated potential rate cuts in 2026. However, concerns over rising oil and gas prices reigniting inflation have altered these expectations.

Swap rates, which influence mortgage pricing, have climbed sharply as markets grapple with this uncertainty. Financial experts now predict that the Bank of England will hold the base rate at 3.75% during its March meeting, a stark contrast to earlier forecasts of a potential reduction. The likelihood of a rate cut this year has fallen from 50% to just 20% in recent days, leaving borrowers in a less favourable position than previously hoped.

Households had been enjoying lower mortgage rates in recent months, but this trend has quickly reversed. French highlighted that the sudden shift is unwelcome news for borrowers, with further increases dependent on global market developments. As lenders continue to adapt to the evolving landscape, homeowners and buyers may wish to stay informed and explore their options carefully in this unpredictable environment.

item.title: How will war in the Middle East affect your finances?
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has sent ripples through global markets, raising concerns about its potential impact on household finances in the UK. With oil prices surging past $100 a barrel for the first time in years and stock markets experiencing volatility, homeowners and savers may be wondering how these developments could affect their budgets, investments, and mortgage costs. Here’s a closer look at the potential implications.

Rising Energy Costs

One of the most immediate effects of the conflict is the rise in oil prices, which could lead to higher energy bills for UK households. As oil becomes more expensive, the cost of producing and transporting goods increases, potentially pushing up prices for petrol, diesel, and household energy. This comes at a time when many families are already grappling with elevated energy costs following the energy crisis of recent years. Homeowners may wish to review their energy tariffs and consider ways to reduce consumption to mitigate the impact.

Impact on Mortgage Rates

The conflict could also influence mortgage rates, albeit indirectly. Global instability often leads to increased market uncertainty, which can affect the Bank of England’s decisions on interest rates. If inflation rises due to higher energy costs, the Bank may respond by maintaining or increasing base rates to curb price growth. This could result in higher mortgage payments for those on variable or tracker rates. Borrowers approaching the end of a fixed-term deal might consider exploring remortgaging options to secure a competitive rate before potential changes.

Investment Volatility

Stock markets have already felt the impact of the conflict, with fluctuations driven by geopolitical tensions and economic uncertainty. Investors may notice increased volatility in their portfolios, particularly in sectors sensitive to oil prices or global trade. Whilst market downturns can be unsettling, they may also present opportunities for long-term investors to buy assets at lower prices. Those concerned about the impact on their investments could consider diversifying their portfolios or seeking advice from a financial adviser.

Whilst the full financial impact of the Middle East conflict remains uncertain, staying informed and proactive can help households navigate potential challenges. Reviewing budgets, exploring energy-saving measures, and keeping an eye on mortgage and investment options could provide some peace of mind during these uncertain times.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has sent ripples through global markets, raising concerns about its potential impact on household finances in the UK. With oil prices surging past $100 a barrel for the first time in years and stock markets experiencing volatility, homeowners and savers may be wondering how these developments could affect their budgets, investments, and mortgage costs. Here’s a closer look at the potential implications.

Rising Energy Costs

One of the most immediate effects of the conflict is the rise in oil prices, which could lead to higher energy bills for UK households. As oil becomes more expensive, the cost of producing and transporting goods increases, potentially pushing up prices for petrol, diesel, and household energy. This comes at a time when many families are already grappling with elevated energy costs following the energy crisis of recent years. Homeowners may wish to review their energy tariffs and consider ways to reduce consumption to mitigate the impact.

Impact on Mortgage Rates

The conflict could also influence mortgage rates, albeit indirectly. Global instability often leads to increased market uncertainty, which can affect the Bank of England’s decisions on interest rates. If inflation rises due to higher energy costs, the Bank may respond by maintaining or increasing base rates to curb price growth. This could result in higher mortgage payments for those on variable or tracker rates. Borrowers approaching the end of a fixed-term deal might consider exploring remortgaging options to secure a competitive rate before potential changes.

Investment Volatility

Stock markets have already felt the impact of the conflict, with fluctuations driven by geopolitical tensions and economic uncertainty. Investors may notice increased volatility in their portfolios, particularly in sectors sensitive to oil prices or global trade. Whilst market downturns can be unsettling, they may also present opportunities for long-term investors to buy assets at lower prices. Those concerned about the impact on their investments could consider diversifying their portfolios or seeking advice from a financial adviser.

Whilst the full financial impact of the Middle East conflict remains uncertain, staying informed and proactive can help households navigate potential challenges. Reviewing budgets, exploring energy-saving measures, and keeping an eye on mortgage and investment options could provide some peace of mind during these uncertain times.

item.title: UK interest rate cuts unlikely this year amid Iran war – and a rise could be ahead
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Financial markets are predicting a significant shift in the trajectory of UK interest rates, with cuts now unlikely this year and the potential for rates to rise in 2025. This marks a stark reversal from earlier forecasts, driven largely by the ongoing conflict between the US and Iran. Investors now anticipate that the Bank of England will hold its base rate at 3.75% for the remainder of 2024, with a possibility of an increase to 4% by June 2025. These developments could have far-reaching implications for UK homeowners and mortgage borrowers.

Market Predictions and Economic Impact

Prior to the escalation of the Iran conflict, there was an 80% chance of a rate cut at the Bank of England's next meeting on 19 March. However, the probability of a hold has now surged to 99%, with policymakers opting to wait and assess how the geopolitical situation unfolds. The uncertainty has led to sharp falls in financial markets, with UK two-year bond yields climbing to 4.129%, their highest level since April 2025. This increase mirrors the volatility seen during Liz Truss’s mini-budget in 2022, when unfunded tax cuts and energy support measures caused bond yields to spike and the pound to plummet.

The conflict has also had a ripple effect on global oil markets, with the Strait of Hormuz—a crucial passage for approximately 20% of the world’s oil supply—effectively closed. Brent crude prices surged to $119 (£89) a barrel before easing slightly to $104 following news of an upcoming G7 finance ministers’ meeting. This instability has contributed to wider economic turbulence, with European stock markets, including the UK’s FTSE 100, experiencing significant losses at the start of the week.

Implications for Mortgage Borrowers

The prospect of sustained or rising interest rates could mean higher mortgage costs for UK homeowners. Mortgage lenders have already begun adjusting their rates in response to market conditions. Data from Moneyfacts reveals that the average two-year fixed residential mortgage rate has risen to 4.87%, up from 4.84% at the end of the previous week. Similarly, the average five-year fixed rate has increased to 4.98%, up from 4.96%. These small but steady rises may add further pressure to households already grappling with the cost of living crisis.

For those nearing the end of their current mortgage deals, it could be worth exploring remortgaging options sooner rather than later. Locking in a fixed rate now may provide some protection against potential future increases. Additionally, homeowners might consider seeking independent financial advice to assess their options in light of the changing economic landscape.

As the situation continues to evolve, borrowers and savers alike will need to stay informed and prepared for further shifts in interest rates and mortgage costs. Keeping a close eye on market developments and seeking professional guidance could help navigate the challenges ahead.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Financial markets are predicting a significant shift in the trajectory of UK interest rates, with cuts now unlikely this year and the potential for rates to rise in 2025. This marks a stark reversal from earlier forecasts, driven largely by the ongoing conflict between the US and Iran. Investors now anticipate that the Bank of England will hold its base rate at 3.75% for the remainder of 2024, with a possibility of an increase to 4% by June 2025. These developments could have far-reaching implications for UK homeowners and mortgage borrowers.

Market Predictions and Economic Impact

Prior to the escalation of the Iran conflict, there was an 80% chance of a rate cut at the Bank of England's next meeting on 19 March. However, the probability of a hold has now surged to 99%, with policymakers opting to wait and assess how the geopolitical situation unfolds. The uncertainty has led to sharp falls in financial markets, with UK two-year bond yields climbing to 4.129%, their highest level since April 2025. This increase mirrors the volatility seen during Liz Truss’s mini-budget in 2022, when unfunded tax cuts and energy support measures caused bond yields to spike and the pound to plummet.

The conflict has also had a ripple effect on global oil markets, with the Strait of Hormuz—a crucial passage for approximately 20% of the world’s oil supply—effectively closed. Brent crude prices surged to $119 (£89) a barrel before easing slightly to $104 following news of an upcoming G7 finance ministers’ meeting. This instability has contributed to wider economic turbulence, with European stock markets, including the UK’s FTSE 100, experiencing significant losses at the start of the week.

Implications for Mortgage Borrowers

The prospect of sustained or rising interest rates could mean higher mortgage costs for UK homeowners. Mortgage lenders have already begun adjusting their rates in response to market conditions. Data from Moneyfacts reveals that the average two-year fixed residential mortgage rate has risen to 4.87%, up from 4.84% at the end of the previous week. Similarly, the average five-year fixed rate has increased to 4.98%, up from 4.96%. These small but steady rises may add further pressure to households already grappling with the cost of living crisis.

For those nearing the end of their current mortgage deals, it could be worth exploring remortgaging options sooner rather than later. Locking in a fixed rate now may provide some protection against potential future increases. Additionally, homeowners might consider seeking independent financial advice to assess their options in light of the changing economic landscape.

As the situation continues to evolve, borrowers and savers alike will need to stay informed and prepared for further shifts in interest rates and mortgage costs. Keeping a close eye on market developments and seeking professional guidance could help navigate the challenges ahead.

item.title: ‘Geopolitical uncertainties’ amid Iran war could slow fall in mortgage rates, says Halifax
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
```html

By Bambang Setiawan

Geopolitical tensions may slow mortgage rate reductions

Halifax has indicated that ongoing conflicts in the Middle East could delay anticipated reductions in mortgage rates this year. The lender, part of Lloyds Banking Group, noted that geopolitical instability may prolong inflationary pressures, potentially influencing the Bank of England's approach to interest rate adjustments.

Recent figures show UK house prices grew by just 0.3% in February to £301,151, marking a notable slowdown from January's 0.8% increase. This deceleration follows the milestone of average property values surpassing £300,000 for the first time earlier this year.

Market expectations adjust amid global uncertainty

Amanda Bryden, Halifax's head of mortgages, commented: "Geopolitical uncertainties appear likely to shape inflation trends and broader economic conditions. In this environment, financial markets now anticipate a more gradual easing of borrowing costs than previously expected."

Mortgage industry analysts observe that expectations for imminent base rate cuts have diminished significantly. Mark Harris of SPF Private Clients noted: "The Middle East conflict has driven energy prices higher while reducing predictions for central bank rate reductions. This has consequently pushed swap rates upwards, directly affecting fixed-rate mortgage pricing."

Several major lenders including HSBC, Nationwide and Coventry Building Society have already adjusted their mortgage rates upwards in response to these market movements. Industry experts suggest other providers may follow suit in coming weeks.

Property market professionals report some potential buyers and sellers adopting a wait-and-see approach amidst the current economic uncertainties. The housing market had shown consistent recovery through February, though recent global events may temper this momentum if financial market volatility persists.

```
item.content:encoded:
```html

By Bambang Setiawan

Geopolitical tensions may slow mortgage rate reductions

Halifax has indicated that ongoing conflicts in the Middle East could delay anticipated reductions in mortgage rates this year. The lender, part of Lloyds Banking Group, noted that geopolitical instability may prolong inflationary pressures, potentially influencing the Bank of England's approach to interest rate adjustments.

Recent figures show UK house prices grew by just 0.3% in February to £301,151, marking a notable slowdown from January's 0.8% increase. This deceleration follows the milestone of average property values surpassing £300,000 for the first time earlier this year.

Market expectations adjust amid global uncertainty

Amanda Bryden, Halifax's head of mortgages, commented: "Geopolitical uncertainties appear likely to shape inflation trends and broader economic conditions. In this environment, financial markets now anticipate a more gradual easing of borrowing costs than previously expected."

Mortgage industry analysts observe that expectations for imminent base rate cuts have diminished significantly. Mark Harris of SPF Private Clients noted: "The Middle East conflict has driven energy prices higher while reducing predictions for central bank rate reductions. This has consequently pushed swap rates upwards, directly affecting fixed-rate mortgage pricing."

Several major lenders including HSBC, Nationwide and Coventry Building Society have already adjusted their mortgage rates upwards in response to these market movements. Industry experts suggest other providers may follow suit in coming weeks.

Property market professionals report some potential buyers and sellers adopting a wait-and-see approach amidst the current economic uncertainties. The housing market had shown consistent recovery through February, though recent global events may temper this momentum if financial market volatility persists.

```
